input.prompt:
I just upgraded my .NET MAUI app from .NET 9 to .NET 10. On iOS, my content
is now going behind the status bar and the Dynamic Island notch area. In .NET 9
I was using `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` and it worked fine. What's the correct
way to handle this in .NET 10? Show me the XAML.

data.content:

input.initialPrompt:
I just upgraded my .NET MAUI app from .NET 9 to .NET 10. On iOS, my content
is now going behind the status bar and the Dynamic Island notch area. In .NET 9
I was using `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` and it worked fine. What's the correct
way to handle this in .NET 10? Show me the XAML.

data.content:
In .NET 10, `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` was replaced with the **cross-platform** `Page.SafeAreaRegions` property. Replace:

```xml
<!-- .NET 9 (removed) -->
<ContentPage xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
```

with:

```xml
<!-- .NET 10 -->
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             SafeAreaRegions="All">
```

`SafeAreaRegions` is a flags enum — you can combine values like `Top`, `Bottom`, `Left`, `Right`, or just use `All`. You can also remove the `xmlns:ios` namespace import if it's no longer needed elsewhere.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI migration issue from .NET 9 to .NET 10 regarding safe area handling on iOS. This is a known breaking change in .NET 10 MAUI.

In .NET 10, the `UseSafeArea` attached property was removed. The replacement is to use padding or the new safe area APIs. Specifically, .NET 10 introduced a new approach using `SafeAreaRegions` property.

Let me recall the details:

In .NET MAUI for .NET 10, the `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` was replaced with a cross-platform `Page.SafeAreaRegions` property. 

The new approach uses a cross-platform `SafeAreaRegions` property that accepts enum values like `None`, `All`, `Top`, `Bottom`, `Left`, and `Right`, replacing the old iOS-specific `UseSafeArea` attached property.
